results.possibleIdentity: Barry Lynn Fuess (1940–2015) was a musician, entertainer, and technician who may be the individual associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Born in Booneville, New York, he was an accomplished guitar player and musician who performed in a musical comedy routine with his brother Louis from the late 1950s through the early 1960s. They also played in a band performing at various venues in Oswego County, New York. During the late 1960s, he served in the U.S. Army Signal Corps in Vietnam, reaching the rank of Specialist 4th Class. The Signal Corps is the branch of the Army responsible for managing communications and information systems, a background that aligns with the technical focus of E.A.T. after his service, he moved to Idaho in the late 1960s and married in 1968. While he worked in construction and as a long-haul truck driver later in life, he remained known for his musical talent and entertaining personality throughout his life.

results.possibleIdentity: Dr. Godfrey Harry Stafford (1920–2013) was a distinguished British physicist and an accomplished artist. He is perhaps the most likely candidate for 'Dr. Stafford' due to his dual career as a top-level scientist and a regularly exhibiting artist during the late 1960s and 1970s. He served as the Director of the Rutherford Laboratory in the UK from 1969 to 1981, overseeing its transition into a multi-disciplinary research center. Alongside his scientific achievements, which included major contributions to particle physics and accelerators at CERN, he was known for his lifelong interest in the arts and was an artist who exhibited his work regularly at the Royal Academy. His career coincided exactly with the peak of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, which promoted the very type of interdisciplinary collaboration between scientists and artists that his life embodied.

Dr. Anthony Stafford Beer (1926–2002), typically known as Stafford Beer, was a world-renowned British cyberneticist and management consultant who was also a prolific artist and poet. Active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was a central figure in the cybernetics movement, which deeply influenced the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and its contemporaries. He participated in the landmark 'Cybernetic Serendipity' exhibition at the ICA in London (1968), contributing a poem titled 'SAM' (Stochastic Analogue Machine), and later produced a notable series of abstract paintings known as the 'Requiem' series. His work frequently explored the intersections of science, technology, and artistic expression, and he was a known correspondent and associate of the artists and engineers in the E.A.T. and USCO circles.

Larry Stafford was an American abstract painter active in the late 1960s and 1970s who was explicitly associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He was one of the artists included in E.A.T.'s major 1968 exhibition, 'Some More Beginnings,' held at the Brooklyn Museum. His work from this period is categorized under 'Lyrical Abstraction' and featured experimental techniques like soft spray painting. He is also documented in the Harry Shunk and Shunk-Kender archive, which primarily focused on the key artists and events of the E.A.T. movement. While he is a confirmed artist within the E.A.T. circle, he is less frequently referred to with the title 'Dr.' compared to the scientific figures of the era.

results.possibleIdentity: Doloris Holmes (later known as Janais) was a performance artist, filmmaker, poet, and playwright active in the New York City art scene from the early 1960s through the late 1990s. She was a significant figure in the feminist art movement, serving as an original member of the activist group Women Artists in Revolution (WAR) and the Art Workers Coalition (AWC). Holmes was the founder and director of the White Mask Theatre (established in New York City), a space dedicated to non-traditional performance, rehearsal, and film projects. Her work often explored 'art as process' and utilized mixed media, including environments, plays, and 16mm films such as 'Room of the White Mask' (1973). In 1970, she participated in the influential all-women 'X-12' exhibition at Museum (a Noho artists' cooperative). She is also documented in the 11th Annual Avant Garde Festival of New York alongside organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In 1972, after a profound experience visiting prehistoric caves in Spain, she created the performance 'Fish-Joy' and eventually divorced her husband, the poet Armand Schwerner. In 1978, she adopted the name 'Janais' to honor Janis Joplin and Anaïs Nin. Holmes conducted notable oral history interviews for the Archives of American Art, including a famous session with Anaïs Nin in 1972. She passed away in June 2001.
